name: Viewport Presence Intent
summary: The shared in/out-of-view trigger: observe when an element enters or leaves a reference frame, owning the observe-vocabulary (reference, margin, coverage, enter/leave) — not the UX decision (fetch, prefetch, activate) that each consumer keeps.

reference.description: The frame an element's presence is measured against (the IntersectionObserver root)

margin.description: How early presence flips relative to the reference edge (the IntersectionObserver rootMargin)

coverage.description: How much of the element must lie within the reference to count as present (the IntersectionObserver threshold)

description:
What Viewport Presence Is

Viewport Presence is the thin, shared in/out-of-view trigger — the answer to "is this element currently within a reference frame?", surfaced as enter/leave events. The native substrate is IntersectionObserver: it owns the observe-vocabulary (root, rootMargin, threshold) and nothing about what happens next.

It owns the trigger, not the UX decision. What a consumer does on presence — fetch the next page, prefetch a link, activate a visibility-gated behavior — stays with that consumer. This intent exists only so the three consumers that today re-implement the same observer (Collection Operations advance:auto, Prefetch eagerness:viewport, and the visibility-gated trait) compose one trigger instead of triplicating it — so a rootMargin-defaulting fix lands once, not three times.

Reference

The frame presence is measured against — the IntersectionObserver root.

  • viewport: Presence is measured against the browser viewport (root: null). The common case.
  • scroll-ancestor: Presence is measured against a scrolling ancestor element (e.g. a scroll container or virtualized list), passed as the observer root.

Margin

How early presence flips relative to the reference edge — the IntersectionObserver rootMargin. The exact offset is a config value the consuming surface (or the platform config it extends) sets; the dimension names the two intents.

  • flush: Presence flips exactly at the reference edge (zero margin) — react when the element is actually on screen.
  • anticipatory: Presence flips before the edge (a positive rootMargin) — fire ahead of arrival so a consumer can fetch or prefetch in advance. The most-flexible, eager framing the pre-trigger consumers want.

Coverage

How much of the element must lie within the reference to count as present — the IntersectionObserver threshold.

  • sliver: Any pixel visible counts (threshold: 0) — the most permissive.
  • partial: A small fraction must be visible before presence flips.
  • majority: More than half must be visible.
  • full: The whole element must be within the reference (threshold: 1).

Events

The two observable transitions every consumer composes:

  • enter: The element became present (crossed into the reference under the active margin + coverage).
  • leave: The element became absent (crossed back out).

Composition

Consumers compose Viewport Presence rather than inline an observer: Collection Operations keeps advance:auto (scroll-to-boundary → fetch the next slice) and delegates only the trigger; Prefetch keeps eagerness:viewport and delegates only the trigger; a visibility-gated trait activates its behavior on enter. The UX decision stays three; the observe-mechanism becomes one.

Interface Protocol

type PresenceReference = 'viewport' | 'scroll-ancestor';type PresenceMargin = 'flush' | 'anticipatory';type PresenceCoverage = 'sliver' | 'partial' | 'majority' | 'full';interface ViewportPresenceIntent {    // The frame presence is measured against (IntersectionObserver root)    reference: PresenceReference;    // How early presence flips vs the reference edge (IntersectionObserver rootMargin)    margin: PresenceMargin;    // How much of the element must be in-frame to count (IntersectionObserver threshold)    coverage: PresenceCoverage;}
